| Unified combatant commands  * [Africa Command](/wiki/United_States_Africa_Command "United States Africa Command") * [Central Command](/wiki/United_States_Central_Command "United States Central Command") * [European Command](/wiki/United_States_European_Command "United States European Command") * [Indo-Pacific Command](/wiki/United_States_Indo-Pacific_Command "United States Indo-Pacific Command") * [Northern Command](/wiki/United_States_Northern_Command "United States Northern Command") * [Southern Command](/wiki/United_States_Southern_Command "United States Southern Command") * [Cyber Command](/wiki/United_States_Cyber_Command "United States Cyber Command") * [Space Command](/wiki/United_States_Space_Command "United States Space Command") * [Special Operations Command](/wiki/United_States_Special_Operations_Command "United States Special Operations Command") * [Strategic Command](/wiki/United_States_Strategic_Command "United States Strategic Command") * [Transportation Command](/wiki/United_States_Transportation_Command "United States Transportation Command")

(**USCG**) is the [maritime security](/wiki/Maritime_security "Maritime security"), [search and rescue](/wiki/Search_and_rescue "Search and rescue"), and [law enforcement](/wiki/Admiralty_law "Admiralty law") [service branch](/wiki/Military_branch "Military branch") of the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces").[[10]](#cite_note-10) It is one of the country's eight [uniformed services](/wiki/Uniformed_services_of_the_United_States "Uniformed services of the United States"). The service is a maritime, military, multi-mission service unique among the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") military branches for having a maritime [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement "Law enforcement") mission with jurisdiction in both domestic and [international waters](/wiki/International_waters "International waters") and a [federal](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") [regulatory agency](/wiki/Regulatory_agency "Regulatory agency") mission as part of its duties. It is the largest [coast guard](/wiki/Coast_guard "Coast guard") in the world.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Maritime law enforcement and rescue service branch of the U.S. military

| United States Coast Guard | |
| --- | --- |
| [Seal](/wiki/Seal_(emblem) "Seal (emblem)") of the United States Coast Guard | |
| Founded | * January 28, 1915 (1915-01-28) (as current service) * August 4, 1790; 235 years ago (1790-08-04) (as the [Revenue-Marine](/wiki/United_States_Revenue_Cutter_Service "United States Revenue Cutter Service"))[[1]](#cite_note-birth_date-1) |
| Country | United States |
| Type | [Coast guard](/wiki/Coast_guard "Coast guard") |
| Role | * [Port and waterway security](/wiki/Port_security "Port security") * [Drug interdiction](/wiki/Drug_interdiction "Drug interdiction") * [Aids to navigation](/wiki/Aids_to_navigation "Aids to navigation") * [Search and rescue](/wiki/Search_and_rescue "Search and rescue") * [Illegal, unreported and unregulated fishing](/wiki/Illegal,_unreported_and_unregulated_fishing "Illegal, unreported and unregulated fishing") * [Marine safety](/wiki/Marine_safety "Marine safety") * [Defense readiness](/wiki/Naval_warfare "Naval warfare") * [Migrant interdiction](/wiki/Border_control "Border control") * [Marine environmental protection](/wiki/Marine_environmental_protection "Marine environmental protection") * [Ice operations](/wiki/Icebreaker "Icebreaker") * [Law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement "Law enforcement") |
| Size | * 40,558 active duty personnel * 7,724 reserve personnel * 21,000 auxiliarists[[2]](#cite_note-Auxiliary_Policy_Statement-2) * 8,577 civilian personnel (as of 2020)[[3]](#cite_note-3) |
| Part of | * [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces")[[4]](#cite_note-4)   + [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security")   + [Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") (during wartime)[[5]](#cite_note-Chapter_8_U.S._Coast_Guard-5)     - [Department of the Navy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Navy "United States Department of the Navy") |
| Headquarters | [Douglas A. Munro Coast Guard Headquarters Building](/wiki/Douglas_A._Munro_Coast_Guard_Headquarters_Building "Douglas A. Munro Coast Guard Headquarters Building"), Washington, D.C., U.S. |
| Nicknames | * "Coasties"[[6]](#cite_note-Benedetto2006-6) * "The Guard"[[6]](#cite_note-Benedetto2006-6) * "[Puddle Pirates](/wiki/Puddle_Pirates "Puddle Pirates")"[[7]](#cite_note-7) |
| Mottos | *Semper Paratus* Always ready[[8]](#cite_note-semper-8) |
| Colors | CG Red, CG Blue, White[[9]](#cite_note-heraldry-9) |
| March | "[Semper Paratus](/wiki/Semper_Paratus_(march) "Semper Paratus (march)")" [Play](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/6/6b/Semper_Paratus_by_USCG_Band.ogg/Semper_Paratus_by_USCG_Band.ogg.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Semper_Paratus_by_USCG_Band.ogg "File:Semper Paratus by USCG Band.ogg") |

The **United States Coast Guard** (**USCG**) is the [maritime security](/wiki/Maritime_security "Maritime security"), [search and rescue](/wiki/Search_and_rescue "Search and rescue"), and [law enforcement](/wiki/Admiralty_law "Admiralty law") [service branch](/wiki/Military_branch "Military branch") of the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces").[[10]](#cite_note-10) It is one of the country's eight [uniformed services](/wiki/Uniformed_services_of_the_United_States "Uniformed services of the United States"). The service is a maritime, military, multi-mission service unique among the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") military branches for having a maritime [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement "Law enforcement") mission with jurisdiction in both domestic and [international waters](/wiki/International_waters "International waters") and a [federal](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") [regulatory agency](/wiki/Regulatory_agency "Regulatory agency") mission as part of its duties. It is the largest [coast guard](/wiki/Coast_guard "Coast guard") in the world.

The U.S. Coast Guard protects the United States' borders and economic and security interests abroad; and defends its sovereignty by safeguarding [sea lines of communication](/wiki/Sea_lines_of_communication "Sea lines of communication") and commerce across U.S. [territorial waters](/wiki/Territorial_waters "Territorial waters") and its [Exclusive Economic Zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone "Exclusive economic zone"). Like its [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") sibling, the U.S. Coast Guard maintains a global presence with permanently-assigned personnel throughout the world and forces routinely deploying to both [littoral](/wiki/Littoral_zone "Littoral zone") and [blue-water](/wiki/Blue-water_navy "Blue-water navy") regions. As a humanitarian service, it provides emergency response and disaster management for a wide range of human-made and natural catastrophic incidents in the U.S. and throughout the world.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The U.S. Coast Guard operates under the [U.S. Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security") during peacetime. During times of war, it can be transferred in whole or in part to the [U.S. Department of the Navy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Navy "United States Department of the Navy") under the [Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") by order of the [U.S. president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") or by act of [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"). Prior to its transfer to Homeland Security, it operated under the [Department of Transportation](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Transportation "United States Department of Transportation") from 1967 to 2003 and the [Department of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") from its inception until 1967.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) A congressional authority transfer to the Navy has only happened once: in 1917, during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").[[14]](#cite_note-NavyTransferWW1-14) By the time the U.S. entered [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in December 1941, the U.S. Coast Guard had already been transferred to the Navy by President [Franklin Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt").[[15]](#cite_note-NavyTransferWW2-15)

The U.S. Coast Guard was formed by a merger of the [U.S. Revenue Cutter Service](/wiki/United_States_Revenue_Cutter_Service "United States Revenue Cutter Service") and the [U.S. Life-Saving Service](/wiki/United_States_Life-Saving_Service "United States Life-Saving Service") on 28 January 1915, under the Department of the Treasury. The Revenue Cutter Service was created by Congress as the Revenue-Marine on 4 August 1790 at the request of [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton"), and is therefore the oldest continuously operating naval service of the United States.[[Note 1]](#cite_note-16) As secretary of the treasury, Hamilton headed the Revenue-Marine, whose original purpose was collecting [customs duties](/wiki/Customs_duties "Customs duties") at U.S. seaports. By the 1860s, the service was known as the U.S. Revenue Cutter Service and the term Revenue-Marine gradually fell into disuse.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

In 1939, the [U.S. Lighthouse Service](/wiki/United_States_Lighthouse_Service "United States Lighthouse Service") was also merged into the U.S. Coast Guard. As one of the country's six armed services, the U.S. Coast Guard and its predecessor have participated in every major U.S. war since 1790, from the [Quasi-War with France](/wiki/Quasi-War "Quasi-War") to the [Global War on Terrorism](/wiki/War_on_Terror "War on Terror").[[17]](#cite_note-USCG01-18)[[18]](#cite_note-Reservist2012-19)

As of December 2021, the U.S. Coast Guard's authorized force strength is 44,500 active duty personnel[[19]](#cite_note-20) and 7,000 reservists.[[Note 2]](#cite_note-21) The service's force strength also includes 8,577 full-time civilian federal employees and 21,000 uniformed civilian volunteers of the [U.S. Coast Guard Auxiliary](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Auxiliary "United States Coast Guard Auxiliary").[[20]](#cite_note-22) The service maintains an extensive fleet of roughly 250 coastal and ocean-going [cutters](/wiki/Cutter_(boat) "Cutter (boat)"), patrol ships, buoy tenders, tugs, and icebreakers; as well as nearly 2,000 small boats and specialized craft. It also maintains an aviation division consisting of more than 200 helicopters and fixed-wing aircraft.[[21]](#cite_note-uscg.mil1-23) While the U.S. Coast Guard is the second smallest of the U.S. military service branches in terms of membership, the service by itself is the world's 12th largest naval force.[[22]](#cite_note-24)[[23]](#cite_note-25)

## Mission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Mission")]

Main article: [Missions of the United States Coast Guard](/wiki/Missions_of_the_United_States_Coast_Guard "Missions of the United States Coast Guard")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Deepwater_Horizon_spill_site_DVIDS1096750.jpg/250px-Deepwater_Horizon_spill_site_DVIDS1096750.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deepwater_Horizon_spill_site_DVIDS1096750.jpg)

A USCG [HH-65 Dolphin](/wiki/HH-65_Dolphin "HH-65 Dolphin") helicopter flies overhead as the Coast Guard Cutter *Resolute* steams near [Deepwater Horizon spill](/wiki/Deepwater_Horizon_spill "Deepwater Horizon spill") site, July 4, 2010.

### Role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Role")]

The Coast Guard carries out three basic roles, which are further subdivided into eleven statutory missions. The three roles are:

* [Maritime safety](/wiki/Bering_Sea_Patrol "Bering Sea Patrol")
* [Maritime security](/wiki/Maritime_security_(USCG) "Maritime security (USCG)")
* [Maritime stewardship](/wiki/Missions_of_the_United_States_Coast_Guard#Protection_of_natural_resources "Missions of the United States Coast Guard")

With a decentralized organization and much responsibility placed on even the most junior personnel, the Coast Guard is frequently lauded for its quick responsiveness and adaptability in a broad range of emergencies. In a 2005 article in *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine following [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina"), the author wrote, "the Coast Guard's most valuable contribution to [a military effort when catastrophe hits] may be as a model of flexibility, and most of all, spirit." Wil Milam, a [rescue swimmer](/wiki/Rescue_swimmer "Rescue swimmer") from [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") told the magazine, "In the Navy, it was all about the mission. Practicing for war, training for war. In the Coast Guard, it was, take care of our people and the mission will take care of itself."[[24]](#cite_note-26)

### Missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Missions")]

The eleven statutory missions as defined by law are divided into [homeland security](/wiki/Homeland_security "Homeland security") missions and non-homeland security missions:[[25]](#cite_note-27)

#### Non-homeland security missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Non-homeland security missions")]

* [Ice operations](/wiki/Icebreaker "Icebreaker"), including the [International Ice Patrol](/wiki/International_Ice_Patrol "International Ice Patrol")
* Living marine resources ([fisheries](/wiki/Fishery "Fishery") [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement "Law enforcement"))
* [Marine environmental protection](/wiki/Marine_environmental_protection "Marine environmental protection")
* [Marine safety](/wiki/Marine_safety_(USCG) "Marine safety (USCG)")
* [Aids to navigation](/wiki/Navigation_aid "Navigation aid")
* [Search and rescue](/wiki/Search_and_rescue "Search and rescue")

#### Homeland security missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Homeland security missions")]

* [Defense readiness](/wiki/DEFCON "DEFCON")
* Maritime [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement_agency "Law enforcement agency")
* [Migrant interdiction](/wiki/Illegal_immigration_to_the_United_States "Illegal immigration to the United States")
* [Ports, waterways and coastal security (PWCS)](/wiki/Port_Security "Port Security")
* [Drug interdiction](/wiki/War_on_Drugs "War on Drugs")

### Search and rescue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Search and rescue")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Coast_Guard_conducts_search_and_rescue_after_Hurricane_Irma_170911-G-VC567-1365.jpg/250px-Coast_Guard_conducts_search_and_rescue_after_Hurricane_Irma_170911-G-VC567-1365.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coast_Guard_conducts_search_and_rescue_after_Hurricane_Irma_170911-G-VC567-1365.jpg)

USCG helicopter, [Rescue swimmers](/wiki/Rescue_swimmer "Rescue swimmer") conduct search and rescue after [Hurricane Irma](/wiki/Hurricane_Irma "Hurricane Irma")

The U.S. Coast Guard Search and Rescue (CG-SAR) is one of the Coast Guard's best-known operations.[[26]](#cite_note-28) The *National Search and Rescue Plan* designates the Coast Guard as the federal agency responsible for maritime SAR operations, and the [United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") as the federal agency responsible for inland SAR.[[27]](#cite_note-29) Both agencies maintain [rescue coordination centers](/wiki/Joint_Rescue_Coordination_Centers "Joint Rescue Coordination Centers") to coordinate this effort, and have responsibility for both military and civilian search and rescue.[[28]](#cite_note-NSARC1-30) The two services jointly provide instructor staff for the National Search and Rescue School that trains SAR mission planners and coordinators. Previously located on Governors Island, New York, the school is now located at [Coast Guard Training Center Yorktown](/wiki/Training_Center_Yorktown "Training Center Yorktown") at Yorktown, Virginia.[[29]](#cite_note-31)

### National Response Center

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: National Response Center")]

Operated by the Coast Guard, the National Response Center (NRC) is the sole [U.S. Government](/wiki/U.S._Government "U.S. Government") point of contact for reporting all [oil](/wiki/Oil_spill "Oil spill"), [chemical](/wiki/Pollution "Pollution"), [radiological](/wiki/Radioactive_contamination "Radioactive contamination"), [biological](/wiki/Pollution "Pollution"), and etiological [spills](/wiki/Pollution "Pollution") and discharges into the environment, anywhere in the United States and its territories.[[30]](#cite_note-32) In addition to gathering and distributing spill/incident information for [Federal On Scene Coordinators](/wiki/Federal_On_Scene_Coordinator "Federal On Scene Coordinator") and serving as the communications and operations center for the National Response Team, the NRC maintains agreements with a variety of federal entities to make additional notifications regarding incidents meeting established trigger criteria. The NRC also takes Maritime Suspicious Activity and Security Breach Reports. Details on the NRC organization and specific responsibilities can be found in the National Oil and Hazardous Substances Pollution Contingency Plan.[[31]](#cite_note-33) [The Marine Information for Safety and Law Enforcement (MISLE)](/wiki/MISLE "MISLE") database system is managed and used by the Coast Guard for tracking pollution and safety incidents in the nation's ports.[[32]](#cite_note-34)[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[34]](#cite_note-36)

### National Maritime Center

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: National Maritime Center")]

The National Maritime Center (NMC) is the merchant mariner credentialing authority for the USCG under the auspices of the [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security"). To ensure a safe, secure, and environmentally sound marine transportation system, the mission of the NMC is to issue credentials to fully qualified mariners in the United States maritime jurisdiction.[[35]](#cite_note-37)

### Authority as an armed service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Authority as an armed service")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Member_of_Coast_Guard_Maritime_Security_Response_Team_West_in_Alaska%2C_April_10%2C_20.jpg/250px-Member_of_Coast_Guard_Maritime_Security_Response_Team_West_in_Alaska%2C_April_10%2C_20.jpg)](/wiki/File:Member_of_Coast_Guard_Maritime_Security_Response_Team_West_in_Alaska,_April_10,_20.jpg)

A member of USCG [Maritime Security Response Team](/wiki/Deployable_Specialized_Forces#Maritime_Security_Response_Team "Deployable Specialized Forces") West trains for a simulated terrorist threat aboard the motor vessel

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/U.S._Sailors_with_a_visit%2C_board%2C_search_and_seizure_team_and_U.S._Coast_Guard_Maritime_Safety_and_Security_Team_91104_prepare_to_attach_a_caving_ladder_to_a_dhow_near_guided-missile_cruiser_USS_Anzio_%28CG_68%29_090908-N-ZL677-060.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:U.S._Sailors_with_a_visit,_board,_search_and_seizure_team_and_U.S._Coast_Guard_Maritime_Safety_and_Security_Team_91104_prepare_to_attach_a_caving_ladder_to_a_dhow_near_guided-missile_cruiser_USS_Anzio_(CG_68)_090908-N-ZL677-060.jpg)

[Maritime Safety and Security Team](/wiki/Maritime_Safety_and_Security_Team "Maritime Safety and Security Team") conduct operations in the Gulf of Aden Sept. 8, 2009 part of [Combined Task Force 151](/wiki/Combined_Task_Force_151 "Combined Task Force 151"), a multinational task force established to conduct counterpiracy operations off the coast of Somalia.

[Title 10 of the U.S. Code](/wiki/Title_10_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 10 of the United States Code") says that "[t]he term "armed forces" means the Army, Marine Corps, Navy, Air Force, Space Force, and Coast Guard."[[36]](#cite_note-38)[[37]](#cite_note-39)
The Coast Guard is further defined by [Title 14 of the United States Code](/wiki/Title_14_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 14 of the United States Code"): "The Coast Guard as established January 28, 1915, shall be a military service and a branch of the armed forces of the United States at all times. The Coast Guard shall be a service in the Department of Homeland Security, except when operating as a service in the Navy."[[38]](#cite_note-40)
Coast Guard organization and operation is as set forth in [Title 33 of the Code of Federal Regulations](/wiki/Title_33_of_the_Code_of_Federal_Regulations "Title 33 of the Code of Federal Regulations").

On 25 November 2002, the [Homeland Security Act](/wiki/Homeland_Security_Act "Homeland Security Act") was signed into law by U.S. President George W. Bush, designating the Coast Guard to be placed under the [U.S. Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security"). The transfer of administrative control from the U.S. Department of Transportation to the U.S. Department of Homeland Security was completed the following year, on 1 March 2003.[[39]](#cite_note-DHSTransfer2003-41)[[40]](#cite_note-42)[[41]](#cite_note-43)

The U.S. Coast Guard reports directly to the civilian secretary of homeland security. However, under [14 U.S.C.](/wiki/Title_14_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 14 of the United States Code") [§ 3](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/14/3) as amended by section 211 of the Coast Guard and Maritime Transportation Act of 2006, upon the declaration of war and when [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") so directs in the declaration, or when the [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") directs, the Coast Guard operates under the Department of Defense as a service in the [Department of the Navy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Navy "United States Department of the Navy").[[42]](#cite_note-44)

As members of the military, coast guardsmen on active and reserve service are subject to the [Uniform Code of Military Justice](/wiki/Uniform_Code_of_Military_Justice "Uniform Code of Military Justice") and receive the same pay and allowances as members of the same pay grades in the other uniformed services.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

The service has participated in every major U.S. conflict from 1790 through today, including landing troops on [D-Day](/wiki/D-Day "D-Day") and on the Pacific Islands in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), in extensive patrols and shore bombardment during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), and multiple roles in [Operation Iraqi Freedom](/wiki/Operation_Iraqi_Freedom "Operation Iraqi Freedom"). Maritime interception operations, coastal security, transportation security, and [law enforcement detachments](/wiki/Law_Enforcement_Detachments "Law Enforcement Detachments") have been its major roles in recent conflicts in [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq").[[44]](#cite_note-46)

On 17 October 2007, the Coast Guard joined with the [U.S. Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") and [U.S. Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") to adopt a new [maritime strategy](/wiki/Maritime_strategy "Maritime strategy") called *[A Cooperative Strategy for 21st Century Seapower](/wiki/A_Cooperative_Strategy_for_21st_Century_Seapower "A Cooperative Strategy for 21st Century Seapower")* that raised the notion of prevention of war to the same philosophical level as the conduct of war.[[45]](#cite_note-Garamone-47) This new strategy charted a course for the Navy, Coast Guard and Marine Corps to work collectively with each other and international partners to prevent regional crises, man-made or natural, from occurring, or reacting quickly should one occur to avoid negative impacts to the United States. During the launch of the new U.S. maritime strategy at the International Seapower Symposium at the [U.S. Naval War College](/wiki/United_States_Naval_War_College "United States Naval War College") in 2007, Coast Guard commandant admiral [Thad Allen](/wiki/Thad_Allen "Thad Allen") said the new maritime strategy reinforced the time-honored missions the service has carried out in the United States since 1790. "It reinforces the Coast Guard maritime strategy of safety, security and stewardship, and it reflects not only the global reach of our maritime services but the need to integrate and synchronize and act with our coalition and international partners to not only win wars ... but to prevent wars," Allen said.[[45]](#cite_note-Garamone-47)

### Authority as a law enforcement agency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Authority as a law enforcement agency")]

USCG Munro [Law Enforcement Detachments](/wiki/Law_Enforcement_Detachments "Law Enforcement Detachments") boarding team interdicts suspected drug smuggling vessel

[14 U.S.C.](/wiki/Title_14_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 14 of the United States Code") [§ 102](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/14/102) authorizes the Coast Guard to enforce U.S. federal laws.[[46]](#cite_note-48) This authority is further defined in [14 U.S.C.](/wiki/Title_14_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 14 of the United States Code") [§ 522](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/14/522), which gives law enforcement powers to all Coast Guard commissioned officers, warrant officers, and petty officers.[[47]](#cite_note-49) Unlike the other armed forces branches, which are prevented from acting in a law enforcement capacity by [18 U.S.C.](/wiki/Title_18_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 18 of the United States Code") [§ 1385](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/18/1385), the [Posse Comitatus Act](/wiki/Posse_Comitatus_Act "Posse Comitatus Act"), and [Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") policy, the Coast Guard is exempt from and not subject to the restrictions of the Posse Comitatus Act.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

Further law enforcement authority is given by [14 U.S.C.](/wiki/Title_14_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 14 of the United States Code") [§ 703](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/14/703) and [19 U.S.C.](/wiki/Title_19_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 19 of the United States Code") [§ 1401](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/19/1401), which empower U.S. Coast Guard active and reserve commissioned officers, warrant officers, and petty officers as federal [customs officers](/wiki/U.S._Customs_and_Border_Protection "U.S. Customs and Border Protection").[[49]](#cite_note-51)[[50]](#cite_note-52) This places them under [19 U.S.C.](/wiki/Title_19_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 19 of the United States Code") [§ 1589a](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/19/1589a), which grants customs officers general federal law enforcement authority, including the authority to:

> (1) carry a firearm;  
> (2) execute and serve any order, warrant, subpoena, summons, or other process issued under the authority of the United States;  
> (3) make an arrest without a warrant for any offense against the United States committed in the officer's presence or for a felony, cognizable under the laws of the United States committed outside the officer's presence if the officer has reasonable grounds to believe that the person to be arrested has committed or is committing a felony; and  
> (4) perform any other law enforcement duty that the Secretary of Homeland Security may designate.

— 19 USC §1589a. Enforcement authority of customs officers[[51]](#cite_note-53)

The U.S. [Government Accountability Office](/wiki/Government_Accountability_Office "Government Accountability Office") Report to the [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), Committee on the Judiciary on its 2006 Survey of Federal Civilian Law Enforcement Functions and Authorities, identified the Coast Guard as one of 104 federal components that employed [law enforcement officers](/wiki/Law_enforcement_officer "Law enforcement officer").[[52]](#cite_note-54) The report also included a summary table of the authorities of the Coast Guard's 192 special agents and 3,780 maritime law enforcement boarding officers.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

Coast Guardsmen have the legal authority to carry their service-issued firearms on and off base. This is rarely done in practice, however; at many Coast Guard stations, commanders prefer to have all service-issued weapons in armories when not in use. Still, one court has held in the case of *People v. Booth* that Coast Guard boarding officers are qualified law enforcement officers authorized to carry personal firearms off-duty for self-defense.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the United States Coast Guard](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_Coast_Guard "History of the United States Coast Guard")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/VTN_Burning_Trawler_sm.jpg/250px-VTN_Burning_Trawler_sm.jpg)](/wiki/File:VTN_Burning_Trawler_sm.jpg)

USCGC patrol boat forces North Vietnamese trawler aground after combat from direct fire ([Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"))

The Coast Guard traced its roots to the small fleet of vessels maintained by the [United States Department of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") beginning in the 1790s to enforce tariffs (an important source of revenue for the new nation). [Secretary of the Treasury](/wiki/Secretary_of_the_Treasury "Secretary of the Treasury") [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton") lobbied Congress to fund the construction of ten [cutters](/wiki/Cutter_(boat) "Cutter (boat)"), which it did on 4 August 1790 (now celebrated as the Coast Guard's official birthday). Until the re-establishment of the Navy in 1798, these "revenue cutters" were the only naval force of the early United States. As such, the cutters and their crews frequently took on additional duties, including combating piracy, rescuing mariners in distress, ferrying government officials, and even carrying mail.[[55]](#cite_note-57) Initially not an organized federal agency at all, merely a "system of cutters," each ship operated under the direction of the customs officials in the port to which it was assigned. Several names, including "Revenue-Marine," were used as the service gradually became more organized. Eventually it was officially organized as the [United States Revenue Cutter Service](/wiki/United_States_Revenue_Cutter_Service "United States Revenue Cutter Service"). In addition to its regular law enforcement and customs duties, revenue cutters and their crews were used to support and supplement the Navy in various armed conflicts including the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War").[[56]](#cite_note-58)

A separate federal agency, the [U.S. Life-Saving Service](/wiki/United_States_Life-Saving_Service "United States Life-Saving Service"), developed alongside the Revenue-Marine. Prior to 1848, there were various charitable efforts at creating systems to provide assistance to shipwrecked mariners from shore-based stations, notably by the [Massachusetts Humane Society](/wiki/Massachusetts_Humane_Society "Massachusetts Humane Society"). The federal government began funding lifesaving stations in 1848 but funding was inconsistent and the system still relied on all-volunteer crews. In 1871, [Sumner Increase Kimball](/wiki/Sumner_Increase_Kimball "Sumner Increase Kimball") was appointed chief of the Treasury Department's newly created Revenue Marine Division, and began the process of organizing the Revenue-Marine cutters into a centralized agency. Kimball also pushed for more funding lifesaving stations and eventually secured approval to create the Lifesaving Service as a separate federal agency, also within the Treasury Department, with fulltime paid crews.

In 1915 these two agencies, the Revenue Cutter Service and the Lifesaving Service, were merged to create the modern United States Coast Guard. The [Lighthouse Service](/wiki/United_States_Lighthouse_Service "United States Lighthouse Service") and the [Bureau of Marine Inspection and Navigation](/wiki/Bureau_of_Marine_Inspection_and_Navigation "Bureau of Marine Inspection and Navigation") were absorbed by the Coast Guard 1939 and 1942 respectively.[[57]](#cite_note-59)[[58]](#cite_note-60)
In 1967, the Coast Guard moved from the [U.S. Department of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") to the newly formed [U.S. Department of Transportation](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Transportation "United States Department of Transportation"), an arrangement that lasted until it was placed under the [U.S. Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security") in 2003 as part of legislation designed to more efficiently protect American interests following the [terrorist attacks of 11 September 2001](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks").[[59]](#cite_note-61)

In times of war, the Coast Guard or individual components of it can operate as a service of the [Department of the Navy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Navy "United States Department of the Navy"). This arrangement has a broad historical basis, as the Coast Guard has been involved in wars as diverse as the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War"), and the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), in which the cutter *Harriet Lane* fired the first naval shots attempting to relieve besieged [Fort Sumter](/wiki/Fort_Sumter "Fort Sumter"). The last time the Coast Guard operated as a whole within the Navy was in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), in all some 250,000 served in the Coast Guard during World War II.[[60]](#cite_note-62) A major fixture of the Coast Guard during WWII were the [Beach Patrol units](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Beach_Patrol "United States Coast Guard Beach Patrol"), responsible for patrolling the shorelines of the United States on-foot and horseback.

[Coast Guard Squadron One](/wiki/Coast_Guard_Squadron_One "Coast Guard Squadron One"), was a combat unit formed by the United States Coast Guard in 1965 for service during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"). Placed under the operational control of the United States Navy, it was assigned duties in [Operation Market Time](/wiki/Operation_Market_Time "Operation Market Time"). Its formation marked the first time since World War II that Coast Guard personnel were used extensively in a combat environment. The squadron operated divisions in three separate areas during the period of 1965 to 1970. Twenty-six [Point-class cutters](/wiki/Point-class_cutter "Point-class cutter") with their crews and a squadron support staff were assigned to the U.S. Navy with the mission of interdicting the movement of arms and supplies from the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea") into [South Vietnam](/wiki/South_Vietnam "South Vietnam") by [Viet Cong](/wiki/Viet_Cong "Viet Cong") and [North Vietnam](/wiki/North_Vietnam "North Vietnam") [junk](/wiki/Junk_(ship) "Junk (ship)") and [trawler](/wiki/Naval_trawler "Naval trawler") operators. The squadron also provided [81mm mortar](/wiki/81mm_mortar "81mm mortar") naval gunfire support to nearby friendly units operating along the South Vietnamese coastline and assisted the U.S. Navy during [Operation Sealords](/wiki/Operation_Sealords "Operation Sealords").[[61]](#cite_note-63)

[Coast Guard Squadron Three](/w/index.php?title=Coast_Guard_Squadron_Three&action=edit&redlink=1 "Coast Guard Squadron Three (page does not exist)"), was a combat unit formed by the United States Coast Guard in 1967 for service during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War").[[62]](#cite_note-64) Placed under the operational control of the United States Navy and based in [Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Pearl_Harbor "Pearl Harbor"). It consisted of five USCG High Endurance Cutters operating on revolving six-month deployments. A total of 35 High Endurance Cutters took part in operations from May 1967 to December 1971, most notably using their 5-inch guns to provide naval gunfire support missions.[[63]](#cite_note-Larzelere124-128-65)

Often units within the Coast Guard operate under [Department of the Navy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Navy "United States Department of the Navy") operational control while other Coast Guard units remain under the [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security").[[64]](#cite_note-66)

### Deployable Operations Group/Deployable Specialized Forces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Deployable Operations Group/Deployable Specialized Forces")]

Main article: [Deployable Specialized Forces](/wiki/Deployable_Specialized_Forces "Deployable Specialized Forces")

The Deployable Operations Group (DOG) was a Coast Guard command established in July 2007. The DOG established a single command authority to rapidly provide the Coast Guard, Department of Homeland Security, Department of Defense, [Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") and other interagency operational commanders adaptive force packages drawn from the Coast Guard's deployable specialized force units. The DOG was disestablished on 22 April 2013 and reorganized into Deployable Specialized Forces (DSF) units that were placed under the control of the Atlantic and Pacific Area Commanders.[[65]](#cite_note-67)

The planning for the unit began after the terrorist attacks of 11 September 2001, and culminated with its formation on 20 July 2007. Its missions included maritime law enforcement, [anti-terrorism](/wiki/Anti-terrorism "Anti-terrorism"), [port security](/wiki/Port_security "Port security"), pollution response, and [diving operations](/wiki/Underwater_diving "Underwater diving").[[66]](#cite_note-68)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There were over 25 specialized units within the Deployable Operations Group including the [Maritime Security Response Team](/wiki/Maritime_Security_Response_Team "Maritime Security Response Team"), [Maritime Safety and Security Teams](/wiki/Maritime_Safety_and_Security_Team "Maritime Safety and Security Team"), Law Enforcement Detachments, [Port Security Units](/wiki/Port_Security_Unit "Port Security Unit"), the [National Strike Force](/wiki/National_Strike_Force "National Strike Force"), and Regional Dive Lockers. The DOG also managed Coast Guard personnel assigned to the Navy Expeditionary Combat Command and was involved in the selection of Coast Guard candidates to attend [Navy BUD/S](/wiki/United_States_Navy_SEAL_selection_and_training "United States Navy SEAL selection and training") and serve with [Navy SEAL Teams](/wiki/United_States_Navy_SEALs "United States Navy SEALs").[[67]](#cite_note-DOG-69)

## Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Organization")]

Main articles: [Organization of the United States Coast Guard](/wiki/Organization_of_the_United_States_Coast_Guard "Organization of the United States Coast Guard") and [List of United States Coast Guard units (2019)](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Coast_Guard_units_(2019) "List of United States Coast Guard units (2019)")

The new Department of Homeland Security headquarters complex is on the grounds of the former [St. Elizabeths Hospital](/wiki/St._Elizabeths_Hospital "St. Elizabeths Hospital") in the [Anacostia](/wiki/Anacostia "Anacostia") section of Southeast Washington, across the [Anacostia River](/wiki/Anacostia_River "Anacostia River") from former Coast Guard headquarters.[[69]](#cite_note-71)

The fiscal year 2016 budget request for the U.S. Coast Guard was [$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")9.96 billion.[[70]](#cite_note-72)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/250704-G-M0101-1001.jpg/250px-250704-G-M0101-1001.jpg)](/wiki/File:250704-G-M0101-1001.jpg)

USCG Districts

### Districts and units

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Districts and units")]

#### Former U.S. Coast Guard Districts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Former U.S. Coast Guard Districts")]

Previously the U.S. Coast Guard had two additional districts, the 15th and 16th, which have since been decommissioned. The 15th district previously encompassed the waters around the [Panama Canal Zone](/wiki/Panama_Canal_Zone "Panama Canal Zone"), as well as the canal itself.[[73]](#cite_note-75) The 16th district was formerly the Philippines.[[74]](#cite_note-76)

### Shore establishments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Shore establishments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Douglas_A._Munro_Coast_Guard_Headquarters_Building_-_Image_5_of_7.jpg/250px-Douglas_A._Munro_Coast_Guard_Headquarters_Building_-_Image_5_of_7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Douglas_A._Munro_Coast_Guard_Headquarters_Building_-_Image_5_of_7.jpg)

The Douglas A. Munro Coast Guard Headquarters building in St. Elizabeths West Campus

Shore establishment commands exist to support and facilitate the mission of the sea and air assets and [Coastal defense](/wiki/Coastal_defense_and_fortification "Coastal defense and fortification"). U.S. Coast Guard Headquarters is located in Southeast Washington, D.C. Examples of other shore establishment types are [Coast Guard Sectors](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Sector "United States Coast Guard Sector") (which may include Coast Guard Bases), Surface Forces Logistics Center (SFLC),[[75]](#cite_note-77) [Coast Guard Stations](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Coast_Guard_stations "List of United States Coast Guard stations"), [Coast Guard Air Stations](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Air_Stations "United States Coast Guard Air Stations"), and the [United States Coast Guard Yard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Yard "United States Coast Guard Yard"). Training centers are included in the shore establishment commands. The military college for the USCG is called the [United States Coast Guard Academy](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Academy "United States Coast Guard Academy")[[76]](#cite_note-78) which trains both new officers through a four year program and enlisted personnel joining the ranks of officers through a 17 week program called Officer Candidate School (OCS). Abbreviated TRACEN, the other Training Centers include [Training Center Cape May](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Training_Center_Cape_May "United States Coast Guard Training Center Cape May") for enlisted bootcamp,[[77]](#cite_note-79) [Training Center Petaluma](/wiki/Training_Center_Petaluma "Training Center Petaluma")[[78]](#cite_note-80) and [Training Center Yorktown](/wiki/Training_Center_Yorktown "Training Center Yorktown")[[79]](#cite_note-81) for enlisted "A" schools and "C" schools, and [Coast Guard Aviation Technical Training Center](/wiki/Coast_Guard_Air_Station_Elizabeth_City "Coast Guard Air Station Elizabeth City")[[80]](#cite_note-82) and [Coast Guard Aviation Training Center Mobile](/wiki/Coast_Guard_Aviation_Training_Center "Coast Guard Aviation Training Center")[[81]](#cite_note-83) for aviation enlisted "A" school, "C" schools, and pilot officer training.

## Personnel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Personnel")]

The Coast Guard has a total workforce of 87,569.[[82]](#cite_note-uscg.mil-84) The formal name for a uniformed member of the Coast Guard is "coast guardsman", irrespective of gender. "Coastie" is an informal term commonly used to refer to current or former Coast Guard personnel. In 2008, the term "guardian" was introduced as an alternative but was later dropped. Admiral [Robert J. Papp Jr.](/wiki/Robert_J._Papp_Jr. "Robert J. Papp Jr.") stated that it was his belief that no Commandant had the authority to change what members of the Coast Guard are called as the term coast guardsman is found in Title 14 USC, which established the Coast Guard in 1915.[[83]](#cite_note-PappMessage-85)[[Note 3]](#cite_note-87) "Team Coast Guard" refers to the four components of the Coast Guard as a whole: Regular, Reserve, Auxiliary, and Coast Guard civilian employees.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Commissioned officers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Commissioned officers")]

Main article: [United States Coast Guard officer rank insignia](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_officer_rank_insignia "United States Coast Guard officer rank insignia")

Commissioned officers in the Coast Guard hold [pay grades](/wiki/Pay_grade "Pay grade") ranging from O-1 to O-10 and have the same rank structure as the Navy.[[85]](#cite_note-ranks-88)[[86]](#cite_note-paygrades-89) Officers holding the rank of ensign (O-1) through lieutenant commander (O-4) are considered junior officers, commanders (O-5) and captains (O-6) are considered senior officers, and rear admirals (O-7) through admirals (O-10) are considered flag officers. The [commandant of the Coast Guard](/wiki/Commandant_of_the_Coast_Guard "Commandant of the Coast Guard") and the [vice commandant of the Coast Guard](/wiki/Vice_Commandant_of_the_Coast_Guard "Vice Commandant of the Coast Guard") are the only members of the Coast Guard authorized to hold the rank of admiral.[[87]](#cite_note-90)

The Coast Guard does not have medical officers or [chaplains](/wiki/Military_Chaplain "Military Chaplain") of its own. Instead, [chaplains from the U.S. Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy_Chaplain_Corps "United States Navy Chaplain Corps"), as well as officers from the [U.S. Public Health Service Commissioned Corps](/wiki/United_States_Public_Health_Service_Commissioned_Corps "United States Public Health Service Commissioned Corps") are assigned to the Coast Guard to perform chaplain-related functions and medical-related functions, respectively. These officers wear Coast Guard uniforms but replace the Coast Guard insignia with that of their own service.[[88]](#cite_note-91)

The Navy and Coast Guard share identical officer rank insignia except that Coast Guard officers wear a gold Coast Guard Shield in lieu of a line star or staff corps officer insignia.

### Warrant officers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Warrant officers")]

Main article: [Warrant officer (United States)](/wiki/Warrant_officer_(United_States) "Warrant officer (United States)")

Highly qualified enlisted personnel in pay grades E-6 through E-9 with a minimum of eight years' experience can compete each year for appointment as warrant officers (WO). Successful candidates are chosen by a board and then commissioned as chief warrant officer two (CWO2) in one of twenty-one specialties. Over time, chief warrant officers may be promoted to chief warrant officer three (CWO3) and chief warrant officer four (CWO4). The ranks of warrant officer (WO1) and chief warrant officer five (CWO5) are not currently used in the Coast Guard. Chief warrant officers may also compete for the Chief Warrant Officer to Lieutenant Program. If selected, the warrant officer will be promoted to lieutenant (O-3E). The "E" designates over four years' active duty service as a warrant officer or enlisted member and entitles the member to a higher rate of pay than other lieutenants.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Enlisted personnel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Enlisted personnel")]

See also: [List of United States Coast Guard enlisted ranks](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Coast_Guard_enlisted_ranks "List of United States Coast Guard enlisted ranks") and [List of United States Coast Guard ratings](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Coast_Guard_ratings "List of United States Coast Guard ratings")

Enlisted members of the Coast Guard have pay grades from E-1 to E-9 and also follow the same rank structure as the Navy. Enlisted members in pay grades of E-4 and higher are considered *[petty officers](/wiki/Petty_officer#United_States "Petty officer")* and follow career development paths very similar to those of Navy petty officers.[[89]](#cite_note-92)

Petty officers in pay grade E-7 and higher are *[chief petty officers](/wiki/Chief_Petty_Officer#United_States "Chief Petty Officer")* and must attend the Coast Guard [Chief Petty Officer Academy](/wiki/Training_Center_Petaluma#Chief_Petty_Officer_Academy "Training Center Petaluma"), or an equivalent Department of Defense school, in order to be advanced to pay grade E-8. The basic themes of the school are:

* Professionalism
* Leadership
* Communications
* Systems thinking and lifelong learning

Enlisted rank insignia is also nearly identical to Navy enlisted insignia. The Coast Guard shield replacing the [petty officer's eagle](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Navy_enlisted_rates#E-4_to_E-6 "List of United States Navy enlisted rates") on collar and cap devices for petty officers or enlisted rating insignia for seamen qualified as a ["designated striker"](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Navy_enlisted_rates#E-1_to_E-3 "List of United States Navy enlisted rates"). Group Rate marks (stripes) for junior enlisted members (E-3 and below) also follow Navy convention with white for seaman, red for fireman, and green for airman. In a departure from the Navy conventions, all petty officers E-6 and below wear red chevrons and all chief petty officers wear gold.[[90]](#cite_note-93)

### Training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Training")]

#### Officer training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Officer training")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Coast_Guard_training_near_North_Head_Lighthouse.jpg/250px-Coast_Guard_training_near_North_Head_Lighthouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coast_Guard_training_near_North_Head_Lighthouse.jpg)

Training exercise at [Cape Disappointment State Park, Washington](/wiki/Cape_Disappointment_State_Park "Cape Disappointment State Park")

The [U.S. Coast Guard Academy](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Academy "United States Coast Guard Academy") is a four-year [service academy](/wiki/United_States_service_academies "United States service academies") located in [New London, Connecticut](/wiki/New_London,_Connecticut "New London, Connecticut"). Approximately 200 cadets graduate each year, receiving a [Bachelor of Science degree](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science "Bachelor of Science") and a commission as an [ensign](/wiki/Ensign_(rank) "Ensign (rank)") in the Coast Guard. Graduates are obligated to serve a minimum of five years on active duty. Most graduates are assigned to duty aboard Coast Guard cutters immediately after graduation, either as Deck Watch Officers (DWOs) or as Engineer Officers in Training (EOITs). Smaller numbers are assigned directly to flight training at [Naval Air Station Pensacola](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Pensacola "Naval Air Station Pensacola"), Florida or to shore duty at Coast Guard [Sector](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Sectors "United States Coast Guard Sectors"), District, or Area headquarters units.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In addition to the Academy, prospective officers, who already hold a college degree, may enter the Coast Guard through [Officer Candidate School](/wiki/Officer_Candidate_School "Officer Candidate School") (OCS), also located at the Coast Guard Academy. OCS is a 17-week course of instruction that prepares candidates to serve effectively as officers in the Coast Guard. In addition to indoctrinating students into a military lifestyle, OCS provides a wide range of highly technical information necessary to perform the duties of a Coast Guard officer.[[91]](#cite_note-95)

Graduates of OCS are usually commissioned as ensigns, but some with advanced graduate degrees may enter as [lieutenants (junior grade)](/wiki/Lieutenant_(junior_grade) "Lieutenant (junior grade)") or [lieutenants](/wiki/Lieutenant "Lieutenant"). Graduating OCS officers entering active duty are required to serve a minimum of three years, while graduating reserve officers are required to serve four years. Graduates may be assigned to a cutter, flight training, a staff job, or an operations ashore billet. OCS is the primary channel through which the Coast Guard enlisted grades ascend to the commissioned officer corps. Unlike the other military services, the Coast Guard does not have a [Reserve Officers' Training Corps](/wiki/Reserve_Officers%27_Training_Corps "Reserve Officers' Training Corps") (ROTC) program.[[92]](#cite_note-96)[[93]](#cite_note-97) However, the [Coast Guard Auxiliary](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Auxiliary "United States Coast Guard Auxiliary") offers the [Coast Guard Auxiliary University Programs](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Auxiliary_University_Programs "United States Coast Guard Auxiliary University Programs") (AUP), which provides college students with leadership training and experience in Coast Guard activities, though participation does not guarantee a commission.[[94]](#cite_note-98) The Coast Guard also operates the Select Reserve Direct Commission program, for prospective candidates interested serving as a Coast Guard Reserve Officer.[[95]](#cite_note-99)

Lawyers, engineers, intelligence officers, military aviators holding commissions in other branches of the U.S. Armed Forces requesting interservice transfers to the Coast Guard, graduates of maritime academies, and certain other individuals may also receive an officer's commission in the Coast Guard through the [Direct Commission Officer](/wiki/Direct_Commission_Officer "Direct Commission Officer") (DCO) program. Depending on the specific program and the background of the individual, the course is three, four or five weeks long. The first week of the five-week course is an indoctrination week. The DCO program is designed to commission officers with highly specialized professional training or certain kinds of previous military experience.[[96]](#cite_note-100)

#### Recruit training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Recruit training")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Flags_Across_America_111105-G-ZR255-029.jpg/250px-Flags_Across_America_111105-G-ZR255-029.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flags_Across_America_111105-G-ZR255-029.jpg)

Recruit companies visiting [Arlington National Cemetery](/wiki/Arlington_National_Cemetery "Arlington National Cemetery") for their one day of off-base liberty, which is their only break in an eight-week boot camp at the [Coast Guard Training Center](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Training_Center_Cape_May "United States Coast Guard Training Center Cape May") in [Cape May, New Jersey](/wiki/Cape_May,_New_Jersey "Cape May, New Jersey")

Newly enlisted personnel are sent to eight weeks of [recruit training](/wiki/Recruit_training "Recruit training") at [Coast Guard Training Center Cape May](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Training_Center_Cape_May "United States Coast Guard Training Center Cape May") in [Cape May, New Jersey](/wiki/Cape_May,_New_Jersey "Cape May, New Jersey"). New recruits arrive at [Sexton Hall](/wiki/Charles_W._Sexton "Charles W. Sexton") and remain there for three days of initial processing which includes haircuts, vaccinations, uniform issue, and other necessary entrance procedures. During this initial processing period, the new recruits are led by temporary company commanders. These temporary company commanders are tasked with teaching the new recruits how to march and preparing them to enter into their designated company. The temporary company commanders typically do not enforce any physical activity such as push ups or crunches. When the initial processing is complete, the new seaman recruits are introduced to their permanent company commanders who will remain with them until the end of training. There is typically a designated lead company commander and two support company commanders. The balance of the eight-week boot camp is spent in learning teamwork and developing physical skills. An introduction of how the Coast Guard operates with special emphasis on the Coast Guard's core values is an important part of the training.

The current nine Recruit Training Objectives are:

* [Self-discipline](/wiki/Self-discipline "Self-discipline")
* Military skills
* [Marksmanship](/wiki/Shooting "Shooting")
* Vocational skills and academics
* Military bearing
* Physical fitness and wellness
* Water survival and swim qualifications
* [Esprit de corps](/wiki/Esprit_de_corps "Esprit de corps")
* [Core values](/wiki/U.S._Coast_Guard#Symbols "U.S. Coast Guard") (Honor, Respect, and Devotion to Duty)[[97]](#cite_note-101)

#### Service schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Service schools")]

Following graduation from recruit training, most members are sent to their first unit while they await orders to attend advanced training in [Class "A" Schools](/wiki/Class_%22A%22_Schools "Class \"A\" Schools"). At "A" schools, Coast Guard enlisted personnel are trained in their chosen [rating](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Coast_Guard_ratings "List of United States Coast Guard ratings"); rating is a Coast Guard and Navy term for enlisted skills synonymous with the Army's and Marine Corps' [military occupation codes](/wiki/United_States_military_occupation_codes "United States military occupation codes") (MOS) and Air Force's [Air Force Specialty Code](/wiki/Air_Force_Specialty_Code "Air Force Specialty Code") (AFSC). Members who earned high [ASVAB](/wiki/ASVAB "ASVAB") scores or who were otherwise guaranteed an "A" School of choice while enlisting may go directly to their "A" School upon graduation from Boot Camp.[[98]](#cite_note-102)

### Civilian personnel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Civilian personnel")]

The Coast Guard employs over 8,577 civilians in over two hundred different job types including [Coast Guard Investigative Service](/wiki/Coast_Guard_Investigative_Service "Coast Guard Investigative Service") [special agents](/wiki/Special_agents "Special agents"), lawyers, engineers, technicians, administrative personnel, tradesmen, and federal firefighters.[[82]](#cite_note-uscg.mil-84)[[99]](#cite_note-103) Civilian employees work at various levels in the Coast Guard to support its various missions.[[100]](#cite_note-104)

## Equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Equipment")]

Main article: [List of equipment of the United States Coast Guard](/wiki/List_of_equipment_of_the_United_States_Coast_Guard "List of equipment of the United States Coast Guard")

### Cutters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Cutters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/USCG_National_Security_Cutter_BERTHOLF_%28WMSL-750%29.jpeg/250px-USCG_National_Security_Cutter_BERTHOLF_%28WMSL-750%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:USCG_National_Security_Cutter_BERTHOLF_(WMSL-750).jpeg)

[USCGC *Bertholf* (WMSL-750)](/wiki/USCGC_Bertholf "USCGC Bertholf"), the first [Legend-class](/wiki/Legend-class_cutter "Legend-class cutter") national security cutters

The Coast Guard operates 243 cutters,[[21]](#cite_note-uscg.mil1-23) defined as any vessel more than 65 feet (20 m) long, that has a permanently assigned crew and accommodations for the extended support of that crew.[[101]](#cite_note-cutterdef-105)

* [National Security Cutter](/wiki/National_Security_Cutter "National Security Cutter") (WMSL): Also known as the Legend-class, these are the Coast Guard's latest class of 418-foot (127 m) cutter. At 418 ft. these are the largest USCG military cutters in active service. One-for-one, Legend-class ships have replaced individually decommissioned 1960s [*Hamilton*-class](/wiki/Hamilton-class_cutter "Hamilton-class cutter") cutters, also known as [High Endurance Cutters](/wiki/High_Endurance_Cutter "High Endurance Cutter") (WHEC). A total of eleven were authorized and budgeted; as of 2021 eight are in service, and two are under construction.
* [Medium Endurance Cutter](/wiki/USCG_medium_endurance_cutter "USCG medium endurance cutter") (WMEC): These are mostly the 210-foot (64 m) *Reliance*-class, and the 270-foot (82 m) Famous-class cutters, although the 283-foot (86 m) [*Alex Haley*](/wiki/USCGC_Alex_Haley "USCGC Alex Haley") also falls into this category. Primary missions are law enforcement, search and rescue, and military defense. [Heritage-class cutters](/wiki/Heritage-class_cutter "Heritage-class cutter") are expected to eventually replace the *Reliance*- and Famous-class cutters as they are completed.[[102]](#cite_note-106)
* [Polar-class](/wiki/Polar-class_icebreaker "Polar-class icebreaker") [icebreaker](/wiki/Icebreaker "Icebreaker") (WAGB): There are three WAGB's used for icebreaking and research though only two, the heavy 399-foot (122 m) [*Polar Star*](/wiki/USCGC_Polar_Star "USCGC Polar Star") and the newer medium class 420-foot (130 m) [*Healy*](/wiki/USCGC_Healy "USCGC Healy"), are active.[[103]](#cite_note-107)[[104]](#cite_note-108)[[105]](#cite_note-109)[[106]](#cite_note-110) [*Polar Sea*](/wiki/USCGC_Polar_Sea "USCGC Polar Sea") is located in Seattle, Washington but is not currently in active service. The icebreakers are being replaced with new heavy icebreakers under the [Polar icebreaker program](/wiki/Polar_Security_Cutter_program "Polar Security Cutter program"), the world's largest coast guard vessel due for delivery in 2025.
* [*USCGC Storis*](/wiki/USCGC_Storis_(WAGB-21) "USCGC Storis (WAGB-21)"): A 360-foot (110 m) icebreaker previously used by [Shell](/wiki/Shell_plc "Shell plc") before being bought by the Coast Guard in December 2024.
* [USCGC *Eagle*](/wiki/USCGC_Eagle_(WIX-327) "USCGC Eagle (WIX-327)"): A 295-foot (90 m) sailing barque used as a training ship for Coast Guard Academy cadets and Coast Guard officer candidates. She was originally built in Germany as [*Horst Wessel*](/wiki/Horst_Wessel#Memorial_namings "Horst Wessel"), and was seized by the United States as a [prize of war](/wiki/Prize_of_war "Prize of war") in 1945.[[107]](#cite_note-111)[[108]](#cite_note-112)
* [USCGC *Mackinaw*](/wiki/USCGC_Mackinaw_(WLBB-30) "USCGC Mackinaw (WLBB-30)"): A 240-foot (73 m) heavy icebreaker built for operations on the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes").
* [Seagoing Buoy Tender](/wiki/USCG_seagoing_buoy_tender "USCG seagoing buoy tender") (WLB): These 225-foot (69 m) ships are used to maintain aids to navigation and also assist with law enforcement and search and rescue.
* [Coastal Buoy Tender](/wiki/USCG_coastal_buoy_tender "USCG coastal buoy tender") (WLM): The 175-foot (53 m) Keeper-class coastal buoy tenders are used to maintain coastal aids to navigation.
* [Sentinel-class](/wiki/Sentinel-class_cutter "Sentinel-class cutter") [cutter](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Cutter "United States Coast Guard Cutter") (WPC): The 154-foot (47 m) Sentinel-class, also known by its program name, the "Fast Response Cutter"-class and is used for search and rescue work and law enforcement.
* [Bay-class](/wiki/Bay-class_tugboat "Bay-class tugboat") [icebreaking tug](/wiki/Icebreaker "Icebreaker") (WTGB): 140-foot (43 m) icebreakers used primarily for domestic icebreaking missions. Other missions include search and rescue, law enforcement, and aids to navigation maintenance.[[109]](#cite_note-113)
* [Patrol Boats](/wiki/Patrol_Boat "Patrol Boat") (WPB): There are two classes of WPBs currently in service; the 110-foot (34 m) [Island-class patrol boats](/wiki/Island-class_patrol_boat "Island-class patrol boat") and the 87-foot (27 m) [Marine Protector-class patrol boats](/wiki/Marine_Protector-class_patrol_boat "Marine Protector-class patrol boat")[[110]](#cite_note-114)[[111]](#cite_note-115)
* [Small Harbor Tug](/wiki/USCG_65%27_Small_harbor_tug "USCG 65' Small harbor tug") (WYTL): 65-foot (20 m) small icebreaking tugboats, used primary for ice clearing in domestic harbors in addition to limited search and rescue and law enforcement roles.

### Boats

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Boats")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/USCG_response_boat_medium_45607_Yorktown.jpg/250px-USCG_response_boat_medium_45607_Yorktown.jpg)](/wiki/File:USCG_response_boat_medium_45607_Yorktown.jpg)

U.S. Coast Guard 45-foot (14 m) Response Boat Medium (RB-M)

The Coast Guard operates about 1,650 boats,[[21]](#cite_note-uscg.mil1-23) defined as any vessel less than 65 feet (20 m) long, which generally operate near shore and on inland waterways.

The Coast Guard boat fleet includes:

* [47-foot Motor Lifeboat](/wiki/47-foot_Motor_Lifeboat "47-foot Motor Lifeboat") (MLB): The Coast Guard's 47-foot (14 m) primary heavy-weather boat used for search and rescue as well as law enforcement and homeland security.
* [Response Boat – Medium](/wiki/Response_Boat_%E2%80%93_Medium "Response Boat – Medium") (RB-M): A new multi-mission 45-foot (14 m) vessel intended to replace the 41-foot (12 m) utility boat. 170 planned
* [Deployable Pursuit Boat](/wiki/USCG_Deployable_Pursuit_Boat "USCG Deployable Pursuit Boat") (DPB): A 38-foot (12 m) launch capable of pursuing fast cocaine smuggling craft.
* [Long Range Interceptor](/wiki/Long_Range_Interceptor "Long Range Interceptor") (LRI): A 36-foot (11 m) high-speed launch that can be launched from the stern ramps of the larger Deepwater cutters.
* [Aids to Navigation Boat](/wiki/Aids_to_Navigation_Boat "Aids to Navigation Boat") (TANB/BUSL/ATON/ANB): Various designs ranging from 26 to 55 feet (7.9 to 16.8 m) used to maintain aids to navigation.
* Special Purpose Craft – Law Enforcement (SPC-LE): Intended to operate in support of specialized law enforcement missions, utilizing three 300 horsepower (220 kW) [Mercury Marine](/wiki/Mercury_Marine "Mercury Marine") engines. The SPC-LE is 33 feet (10 m) long and capable of speeds in excess of 50 knots (93 km/h; 58 mph) and operations more than 30 miles (48 km) from shore.
* [29-foot Response Boat Small II](/wiki/Response_boat_-_Small_II "Response boat - Small II") (RBS-II): The successor to the [25-foot RB-S](/wiki/Defender-class_boat "Defender-class boat"), the RBS-II is a 29 foot (9 m) high speed, multi-mission boat commonly used for search and rescue, [port security](/wiki/Port_security "Port security"), and law enforcement. Improvements from the RB-S include improved visibility and modernized electronic chart plotter capabilities.
* [25-foot Transportable Port Security Boat](/wiki/25-foot_Transportable_Port_Security_Boat "25-foot Transportable Port Security Boat") (TPSB): A 25-foot (7.6 m) well-armed boat used by [Port Security Units](/wiki/Port_Security_Unit "Port Security Unit") for force protection.
* Special Purpose Craft, Shallow-water (SPC-SW): 24 feet (7.3 m)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]
* [Cutter Boat – Over the Horizon](/wiki/Cutter_Boat_%E2%80%93_Over_the_Horizon "Cutter Boat – Over the Horizon") (OTH): A 23-foot (7.0 m) rigid hull inflatable boat used by medium and high endurance cutters and specialized units.
* [Short Range Prosecutor (SRP)](/wiki/Short_Range_Prosecutor "Short Range Prosecutor"): A 23-foot (7.0 m) rigid hull inflatable boat that can be launched from a stern launching ramp on the National Security Cutters.

### Aircraft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Aircraft")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Coast_Guard_Airframe_Fleet.jpg/250px-Coast_Guard_Airframe_Fleet.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coast_Guard_Airframe_Fleet.jpg)

Every aircraft type in the U.S. Coast Guard fleet in June 2024. From left: [HC-130J](/wiki/HC-130J "HC-130J"), [C-27J](/wiki/Alenia_C-27J_Spartan "Alenia C-27J Spartan"), [HC-144](/wiki/EADS_HC-144_Ocean_Sentry "EADS HC-144 Ocean Sentry"), [C-37B](/wiki/C-37B "C-37B"), [MH-60T](/wiki/MH-60T "MH-60T"), [MH-65E](/wiki/Eurocopter_MH-65_Dolphin "Eurocopter MH-65 Dolphin")

The Coast Guard operates approximately 201 [fixed and rotary wing aircraft](/wiki/List_of_active_United_States_military_aircraft#Coast_Guard "List of active United States military aircraft")[[21]](#cite_note-uscg.mil1-23) from 24 [Coast Guard Air Stations](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Air_Stations "United States Coast Guard Air Stations") throughout the contiguous United States, Alaska, Hawaii, and Puerto Rico. Most of these air stations are tenant activities at civilian airports, several of which are former Air Force Bases and Naval Air Stations, although several are also independent military facilities. Coast Guard Air Stations are also located on active Naval Air Stations, [Air National Guard](/wiki/Air_National_Guard "Air National Guard") bases, and Army Air Fields.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Coast Guard aviators receive Primary (fixed-wing) and Advanced (fixed or rotary-wing) flight training with their Navy and Marine Corps counterparts at [NAS Whiting Field](/wiki/NAS_Whiting_Field "NAS Whiting Field"), Florida, and [NAS Corpus Christi](/wiki/NAS_Corpus_Christi "NAS Corpus Christi"), Texas, and are considered [Naval Aviators](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Aviator "United States Naval Aviator"). After receiving [Naval Aviator Wings](/wiki/Naval_Aviator_insignia "Naval Aviator insignia"), Coast Guard pilots, with the exception of those slated to fly the HC-130, report to [U.S. Coast Guard Aviation Training Center, Mobile, Alabama](/wiki/Coast_Guard_Aviation_Training_Center "Coast Guard Aviation Training Center") to receive 6–12 weeks of specialized training in the Coast Guard fleet aircraft they will operate. HC-130 pilots report to [Little Rock AFB](/wiki/Little_Rock_AFB "Little Rock AFB"), Arkansas, for joint C-130 training under the auspices of the [314th Airlift Wing](/wiki/314th_Airlift_Wing "314th Airlift Wing") of the [U.S. Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Fixed-wing aircraft operate from Air Stations on long-duration missions. Helicopters operate from Air Stations and can deploy on a number of different cutters. Helicopters can rescue people or intercept vessels smuggling migrants or narcotics. Since the terrorist attacks of 11 September 2001, the Coast Guard has developed a more prominent role in national security and now has armed helicopters operating in high-risk areas for the purpose of maritime law enforcement and anti-terrorism.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Coast Guard is now developing an [unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)](/wiki/Unmanned_aerial_vehicle "Unmanned aerial vehicle") program that will utilize the [MQ-9 Reaper](/wiki/MQ-9_Guardian "MQ-9 Guardian") platform for homeland security and search/rescue operations. To support this endeavor, the Coast Guard has partnered with the Navy and [U.S. Customs and Border Protection](/wiki/U.S._Customs_and_Border_Protection "U.S. Customs and Border Protection") to study existing/emerging unmanned aerial system (UAS) capabilities within their respective organizations. As these systems mature, research and operational experience gleaned from this joint effort will enable the Coast Guard to develop its own cutter and land-based UAS capabilities.[[112]](#cite_note-116)

#### Naval guns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Naval guns")]

Most Coast Guard Cutters have one or more naval gun systems installed, including:

* The [Oto Melara 76 mm](/wiki/Oto_Melara_76_mm "Oto Melara 76 mm"), a radar-guided computer controlled gun system that is used on Medium Endurance Cutters. The 3-inch gun's high rate of fire and availability of specialized ammunition make it a multi-purpose gun capable of anti-shipping, anti-aircraft, ground support, and short-range anti-missile defense.
* The MK 110 57 mm gun, a radar-guided computer controlled variant of the [Bofors 57 mm gun](/wiki/Bofors_57_mm_L/70_naval_artillery_gun "Bofors 57 mm L/70 naval artillery gun"). It is used on the [Legend-class cutter, also known as the National Security Cutter (NSC)](/wiki/Legend-class_cutter "Legend-class cutter"). It is a multi-purpose gun capable of anti-shipping, anti-aircraft, and short-range anti-missile defense. The stealth mount has a reduced radar profile. Also, the gun has a small radar mounted on the gun barrel to measure muzzle velocity for fire control purposes and can change ammunition types instantly due to a dual-feed system. It can also be operated/fired manually using a joystick and video camera (mounted on gun).
* The [Mk 38 Mod 0](/wiki/M242_Bushmaster "M242 Bushmaster") weapons system consists of an M242 Bushmaster 25mm chain gun and the Mk 88 Mod 0 machine gun mount. A manned system, its gyro-stabilization compensates for the pitching deck. It provides ships with defensive and offensive gunfire capability for the engagement of a variety of surface targets. Designed primarily as a close-range defensive measure, it provides protection against patrol boats, floating mines, and various shore-based targets.
* The [Mk 38 Mod 2](/wiki/M242_Bushmaster "M242 Bushmaster") weapons system is a remotely operated Mk 38 with an electronic optical sight, laser range-finder, [FLIR](/wiki/FLIR "FLIR"), a more reliable feeding system, all of which enhance the weapon systems capabilities and accuracy.
* The [Phalanx CIWS](/wiki/Phalanx_CIWS "Phalanx CIWS") (pronounced "sea-wiz") is a close-in weapon system for defense against aircraft and anti-ship missiles. it can also be used against a variety of surface targets. Consisting of a radar-guided 20 mm 6-barreled [M61 Vulcan](/wiki/M61_Vulcan "M61 Vulcan") cannon mounted on a swiveling base, it is used on the Coast Guard's National Security Cutters. This system can operate autonomously against airborne threats or may be manually operated with the use of electronic optical sight, laser range-finder and FLIR systems against surface targets.
* The [Sea PROTECTOR MK50](/wiki/CROWS "CROWS") is a remotely controlled gyro-stabilized [M2 .50 caliber heavy machine gun](/wiki/M2_Browning_machine_gun "M2 Browning machine gun"). The sight package includes a daylight video camera, a thermal camera and an eye-safe laser rangefinder operated by a joystick. It is also furnished with a fully integrated fire control system that provides ballistic correction. The Mk50s are used on only four [Marine Protector-class](/wiki/Marine_Protector-class_patrol_boat "Marine Protector-class patrol boat") Cutters, the [USCGC *Sea Fox* (WPB-87374)](/wiki/USCGC_Sea_Fox "USCGC Sea Fox"), [USCGC *Sea Devil* (WPB-87368)](/wiki/USCGC_Sea_Devil "USCGC Sea Devil"), [USCGC *Sea Dragon* (WPB-87367)](/wiki/USCGC_Sea_Dragon "USCGC Sea Dragon") and [USCGC *Sea Dog* (WPB-87373)](/w/index.php?title=USCGC_Sea_Dog_(WPB-87373)&action=edit&redlink=1 "USCGC Sea Dog (WPB-87373) (page does not exist)")
