## Symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Symbols")]

### Core values

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Core values")]

The Coast Guard, like the other armed services of the United States, has a set of core values that serve as basic ethical guidelines for all Coast Guard active duty, reservists, auxiliarists, and civilians. The **Coast Guard Core Values** are:

> **Honor**: Integrity is our standard. We demonstrate uncompromising ethical conduct and moral behavior in all of our personal actions. We are loyal and accountable to the public trust.  
> **Respect**: We value our diverse workforce. We treat each other with fairness, dignity, and compassion. We encourage individual opportunity and growth. We encourage creativity through empowerment. We work as a team.  
> **Devotion to Duty**: We are professionals, military and civilian, who seek responsibility, accept accountability, and are committed to the successful achievement of our organizational goals. We exist to serve. We serve with pride.
>
> [[115]](#cite_note-CGCV-119)

### The Guardian Ethos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: The Guardian Ethos")]

In 2008, the Coast Guard introduced the Guardian Ethos. As the commandant, Admiral Allen noted in a message to all members of the Coast Guard: [The Ethos] "defines the essence of the Coast Guard," and is the "contract the Coast Guard and its members make with the nation and its citizens."[[116]](#cite_note-120)

### The Coast Guard Ethos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: The Coast Guard Ethos")]

In an ALCOAST message effective 1 December 2011 the Commandant, Admiral Papp, directed that the language of *Guardian Ethos* be superseded by the *Coast Guard Ethos* in an effort to use terminology that would help with the identity of personnel serving in the Coast Guard.[[117]](#cite_note-121) The term *coast guardsman* is the correct form of address used in Title 14 USC and is the form that has been used historically. This changed the line in the Guardian Ethos "I am a Guardian." to become "I am a Coast Guardsman."[[118]](#cite_note-122)

The Ethos is:

> In Service to our Nation   
> With Honor, Respect, and Devotion to Duty   
> We protect   
> We defend   
> We save   
> We are Semper Paratus   
> We are the United States Coast Guard

— The Coast Guard Ethos[[84]](#cite_note-ALCOAST_554/11-86)

### Creed of the United States Coast Guardsman

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Creed of the United States Coast Guardsman")]

Main article: [Creed of the United States Coast Guardsman](/wiki/Creed_of_the_United_States_Coast_Guardsman "Creed of the United States Coast Guardsman")

The "Creed of the United States Coast Guardsman" was written by Vice Admiral [Harry G. Hamlet](/wiki/Harry_G._Hamlet "Harry G. Hamlet"), who served as Commandant of the Coast Guard from 1932 to 1936.[[119]](#cite_note-123)

> I am proud to be a United States Coast Guardsman.  
> I revere that long line of expert seamen who by their devotion to duty and sacrifice of self have made it possible for me to be a member of a service honored and respected, in peace and in war, throughout the world.  
> I never, by word or deed, will bring reproach upon the fair name of my service, nor permit others to do so unchallenged.  
> I will cheerfully and willingly obey all lawful orders.  
> I will always be on time to relieve, and shall endeavor to do more, rather than less, than my share.  
> I will always be at my station, alert and attending to my duties.  
> I shall, so far as I am able, bring to my seniors solutions, not problems.  
> I shall live joyously, but always with due regard for the rights and privileges of others.  
> I shall endeavor to be a model citizen in the community in which I live.  
> I shall sell life dearly to an enemy of my country, but give it freely to rescue those in peril.  
> With God's help, I shall endeavor to be one of His noblest Works...  
> A UNITED STATES COAST GUARDSMAN.

— Creed of the United States Coast Guardsman[[120]](#cite_note-124)

### "You have to go out, but you don't have to come back!"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: \"You have to go out, but you don't have to come back!\"")]

This unofficial motto of the Coast Guard dates to an 1899 [United States Lifesaving Service](/wiki/United_States_Lifesaving_Service "United States Lifesaving Service") regulation, which states in part: "In attempting a rescue, ... he will not desist from his efforts until by actual trial, the impossibility of effecting a rescue is demonstrated. The statement of the keeper that he did not try to use the boat because the sea or surf was too heavy will not be accepted, unless attempts to launch it were actually made and failed."[[121]](#cite_note-125)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Ensign_of_the_United_States_Coast_Guard_%281915%E2%80%931953%29.png/250px-Ensign_of_the_United_States_Coast_Guard_%281915%E2%80%931953%29.png)](/wiki/File:Ensign_of_the_United_States_Coast_Guard_(1915%E2%80%931953).png)

Former Coast Guard ensign, used from 1915 to 1953

The Coast Guard [ensign](/wiki/Ensign_(flag) "Ensign (flag)") (flag) was first flown by the [Revenue Cutter Service](/wiki/Revenue_Cutter_Service "Revenue Cutter Service") in 1799 to distinguish revenue cutters from merchant ships. A 1 August 1799 order issued by Secretary of the Treasury [Oliver Wolcott Jr.](/wiki/Oliver_Wolcott_Jr. "Oliver Wolcott Jr.") specified that the Ensign would be "sixteen perpendicular stripes (for the number of states in the United States at the time), alternate red and white, the union of the ensign to be the arms of the United States in a dark blue on a white field."[[122]](#cite_note-126)

This ensign became familiar in American waters and served as the sign of authority for the Revenue Cutter Service until the early 20th century. The ensign was originally intended to be flown only on revenue cutters and boats connected with the Customs Service but over the years it was found flying atop custom houses as well, and the practice became a requirement in 1874. On 7 June 1910, President [William Howard Taft](/wiki/William_Howard_Taft "William Howard Taft") issued an executive order adding an emblem to (or "[defacing](/wiki/Defacement_(flag) "Defacement (flag)")") the ensign flown by the Revenue cutters to distinguish it from what is now called the [Customs Ensign](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_United_States_Customs_Service.svg "File:Flag of the United States Customs Service.svg") flown from the custom houses. The emblem was changed to the official seal of the Coast Guard in 1927.[[123]](#cite_note-127)[[124]](#cite_note-128)

The purpose of the ensign is to allow ship captains to easily recognize those vessels having legal authority to stop and board them. It is flown only as a symbol of law enforcement authority and is never carried as a parade standard.[[125]](#cite_note-129)

### Coast Guard standard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Coast Guard standard")]

The Coast Guard standard is used in parades and carries the battle honors of the Coast Guard. It was derived from the [jack](/wiki/Maritime_flags#Jacks "Maritime flags") of the Coast Guard ensign which was flown by revenue cutters. The emblem is a blue eagle from the coat of arms of the United States on a white field. Above the eagle are the words "United States Coast Guard" below the eagle is the motto, "Semper Paratus" and the inscription "1790."[[126]](#cite_note-130)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Flickr_-_Official_U.S._Navy_Imagery_-_Indonesian_and_U.S._Coast_Guard_ships_transit_the_Java_Sea_while_conducting_ship_formation_exercises_during_the_at-sea_phase_of_Cooperation_Afloat_Readiness_and_Training_2012_Indonesia._%281%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_Official_U.S._Navy_Imagery_-_Indonesian_and_U.S._Coast_Guard_ships_transit_the_Java_Sea_while_conducting_ship_formation_exercises_during_the_at-sea_phase_of_Cooperation_Afloat_Readiness_and_Training_2012_Indonesia._(1).jpg)

The Service Mark was developed to distinguish Coast Guard cutters from other government and commercial ships.

### Service Mark ("Racing Stripe")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Service Mark (\"Racing Stripe\")")]

The Racing Stripe, officially known as the Service Mark, was designed in 1964 by the industrial design office of [Raymond Loewy](/wiki/Raymond_Loewy "Raymond Loewy") Associates to give the Coast Guard a distinctive, modern image. Loewy had designed the colors for the [Air Force One](/wiki/Air_Force_One "Air Force One") fleet for [Jackie Kennedy](/wiki/Jackie_Kennedy "Jackie Kennedy"). President [Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") was so impressed with his work, he suggested that the entire Federal Government needed his make-over and suggested that he start with the Coast Guard.[[127]](#cite_note-131)[[128]](#cite_note-132) The stripes are canted at a 64-degree angle, coincidentally the year the Racing Stripe was designed.[[129]](#cite_note-133)

The racing stripe is borne by Coast Guard cutters, aircraft, and many boats. First used and placed into official usage as of 6 April 1967, it consists of a narrow blue stripe, a narrow white stripe between, and a broad CG red bar with the Coast Guard shield centered.[[9]](#cite_note-heraldry-9)[[130]](#cite_note-134) Red-hulled icebreaker cutters and most HH-65/MH-65 helicopters (i.e., those with a red fuselage) bear a narrow blue stripe, a narrow empty stripe the color of the fuselage (an implied red stripe), and broad white bar, with the Coast Guard shield centered. Conversely, black-hulled cutters (such as buoy tenders and inland construction tenders) use the standard racing stripe. Auxiliary vessels maintained by the Coast Guard also carry the Racing Stripe, but in inverted colors (i.e., broad blue stripe with narrow white and CG red stripes) and the Auxiliary shield. Similar racing stripe designs have been adopted for the use of other coast guards and maritime authorities and many other law enforcement and rescue agencies.[[Note 5]](#cite_note-135)

## Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/USCG_Uniforms.png/250px-USCG_Uniforms.png)](/wiki/File:USCG_Uniforms.png)

Photo showing a variety of Coast Guard uniforms. From Left: Service Dress White, Tropical Blue, Service Dress Blue, Winter Dress Blue, Camouflage Utility Uniform, Operational Dress Uniform

Main article: [Uniforms of the United States Coast Guard](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_United_States_Coast_Guard "Uniforms of the United States Coast Guard")

For most of the Coast Guard's history, its uniforms largely mirrored the style of U.S. Navy uniforms, distinguishable only by their insignia. In 1974, under the leadership of Admiral [Chester R. Bender](/wiki/Chester_R._Bender "Chester R. Bender"), the initial versions of the current Coast Guard Service Dress Blue and Tropical uniforms were introduced. This represented a major departure from many common conventions in naval and maritime uniforms. Notably, "Bender's Blues" was a common service dress uniform for all ranks, dispensing with the [sailor suit](/wiki/Sailor_suit "Sailor suit") and [sailor cap](/wiki/Sailor_cap "Sailor cap") formerly worn by enlisted members.[[131]](#cite_note-Regs-136) Rank insignia remained consistent with the naval pattern and some distinctly-nautical items such as the [pea coat](/wiki/Pea_coat "Pea coat"), officer's sword, and dress white uniforms remained.[[131]](#cite_note-Regs-136)

Today, the Coast Guard's uniforms remain among the simplest of any branch of the armed forces, with fewer total uniforms and uniform variants than the other armed services. There are only three uniforms that typically serve as standard uniforms of the day—the Operational Dress Uniform, Tropical Blue, and Service Dress Blue (Bravo).[[132]](#cite_note-137)

## Coast Guard Reserve

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Coast Guard Reserve")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/USCG_TPSB.jpg/250px-USCG_TPSB.jpg)](/wiki/File:USCG_TPSB.jpg)

A USCG [Port Security Unit](/wiki/Port_Security_Unit "Port Security Unit") boat patrolling in [Umm Qasr](/wiki/Umm_Qasr "Umm Qasr"), Iraq in 2003

Main article: [United States Coast Guard Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Reserve "United States Coast Guard Reserve")

The [United States Coast Guard Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Reserve "United States Coast Guard Reserve") is the reserve military force of the Coast Guard.[[133]](#cite_note-14USC704-138) The Coast Guard Reserve was founded on 19 February 1941. The Coast Guard has 8700 reservists[[82]](#cite_note-uscg.mil-84) who normally drill two days a month and an additional 12 days of active duty each year, although many perform additional drill and active duty periods, to include those mobilized to extended active duty. Coast Guard reservists possess the same training and qualifications as their active duty counterparts, and as such, can be found augmenting active duty Coast Guard units every day.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") and shortly thereafter, the Coast Guard considered abandoning the reserve program, but the force was instead reoriented into force augmentation, where its principal focus was not just reserve operations, but to add to the readiness and mission execution of every-day active duty personnel.[[134]](#cite_note-139)

Since 11 September 2001, reservists have been activated and served on tours of active duty, to include deployments to the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") and also as parts of Department of Defense combatant commands such as the [U.S. Northern](/wiki/United_States_Northern_Command "United States Northern Command") and [Central](/wiki/United_States_Central_Command "United States Central Command") Commands. Coast Guard [Port Security Units](/wiki/Port_Security_Unit "Port Security Unit") are entirely staffed with reservists, except for five to seven active duty personnel. Additionally, most of the staffing the Coast Guard provides to the [Navy Expeditionary Combat Command](/wiki/Navy_Expeditionary_Combat_Command "Navy Expeditionary Combat Command") are reservists.[[135]](#cite_note-140)

The Reserve is managed by the Assistant Commandant for Reserve, Rear Admiral James M. Kelly, USCG.[[136]](#cite_note-141)

## Women in the Coast Guard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Women in the Coast Guard")]

Main article: [Women in the United States Coast Guard](/wiki/Women_in_the_United_States_Coast_Guard "Women in the United States Coast Guard")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/SPARS_-_NARA_-_515462.jpg/250px-SPARS_-_NARA_-_515462.jpg)](/wiki/File:SPARS_-_NARA_-_515462.jpg)

[SPARS](/wiki/SPARS "SPARS") recruiting poster during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")

There have been women in the United States Coast Guard since 1918, and women continue to serve in it today.[[137]](#cite_note-cbc.ca-142)[[138]](#cite_note-WHC-143)[[139]](#cite_note-womensmemorial-144)

During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), in January 1918, radio and telegraph operator [Myrtle Hazard](/wiki/Myrtle_Hazard "Myrtle Hazard") enlisted as an electrician. She was the only woman to serve during the war and she is the namesake of [USCGC *Myrtle Hazard* .](/wiki/USCGC_Myrtle_Hazard "USCGC Myrtle Hazard")[[140]](#cite_note-145) While some newspapers reported that twin sisters Genevieve and Lucille Baker were the first women to serve in the Coast Guard, their attempt to enlist was rejected.[[141]](#cite_note-146)

## Coast Guard Auxiliary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Coast Guard Auxiliary")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/AUX_W.svg/250px-AUX_W.svg.png)](/wiki/File:AUX_W.svg)

Badge of the [United States Coast Guard Auxiliary](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Auxiliary "United States Coast Guard Auxiliary")

Main article: [United States Coast Guard Auxiliary](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Auxiliary "United States Coast Guard Auxiliary")

The [United States Coast Guard Auxiliary](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Auxiliary "United States Coast Guard Auxiliary") is the uniformed, civilian volunteer component of the United States Coast Guard, created on 23 June 1939 by an act of Congress.[[142]](#cite_note-Title14-147) Although it is a civilian organization, it was originally named the "[United States Coast Guard Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Reserve "United States Coast Guard Reserve")" and was later re-named the "United States Coast Guard Auxiliary" on 19 February 1941 when a [military reserve force](/wiki/Military_reserve_force "Military reserve force") for the Coast Guard was created. As part of "Team Coast Guard" (the term used to collectively describe all active, reserve, auxiliary, and civilian employees), the auxiliary carries out, or assists in, nearly all of the Coast Guard's noncombatant and non-law enforcement missions.[[143]](#cite_note-148) Auxiliarists are subject to direction from the commandant of the Coast Guard. As of 2022, there were approximately 26,000 members of the U.S. Coast Guard Auxiliary.[[144]](#cite_note-149)

Coast Guard policy has assigned many of its duties related to recreational boating safety to the Auxiliary, including public boating safety education and outreach. This includes offering boating skills courses, liaising with marine-related businesses at the local level, and providing voluntary Vessel Safety Checks (formerly called Courtesy Examinations) to the public.[[145]](#cite_note-150) Additionally, Auxiliarists use their own vessels, boats, and aircraft (once registered as Coast Guard facilities) to provide operational support to the Coast Guard by conducting safety patrols, assisting in search and rescue missions, inspecting [aids to navigation](/wiki/Aids_to_navigation "Aids to navigation"), and performing other tasks on behalf of the Coast Guard.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Prior to 1997, Auxiliarists were largely limited to activities supporting recreational boating safety. In 1997, however, new legislation authorized the Auxiliary to participate in any and all Coast Guard missions except direct military and direct law enforcement.[[146]](#cite_note-151) Auxiliarists may directly augment active duty Coast Guard personnel in non-combat, non-law enforcement roles (e.g. radio communications watch stander, interpreter, cook, etc.) and may assist active duty personnel in inspecting commercial vessels and maintaining aids-to-navigation. Auxiliarists may support the law enforcement and homeland security missions of the Coast Guard but may not directly participate (make arrests, etc.), and Auxiliarists are not permitted to carry a weapon while serving in any Auxiliary capacity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[147]](#cite_note-152)

## Medals and honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Medals and honors")]

Main article: [Awards and decorations of the United States Coast Guard](/wiki/Awards_and_decorations_of_the_United_States_Coast_Guard "Awards and decorations of the United States Coast Guard")

See also: [Awards and decorations of the United States military](/wiki/Awards_and_decorations_of_the_United_States_military "Awards and decorations of the United States military")

One Coast Guardsman, [Douglas Albert Munro](/wiki/Douglas_Albert_Munro "Douglas Albert Munro"), has earned the [Medal of Honor](/wiki/Medal_of_Honor "Medal of Honor"), the highest military award of the United States.[[148]](#cite_note-Munro-153) Fifty-five coast guardsmen have earned the [Navy Cross](/wiki/Navy_Cross "Navy Cross") and numerous men and women have earned the [Distinguished Flying Cross](/wiki/Distinguished_Flying_Cross_(USA) "Distinguished Flying Cross (USA)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The highest peacetime decoration awarded within the Coast Guard is the [Homeland Security Distinguished Service Medal](/wiki/Homeland_Security_Distinguished_Service_Medal "Homeland Security Distinguished Service Medal"); prior to the transfer of the Coast Guard to the Department of Homeland Security, the highest peacetime decoration was the [Department of Transportation Distinguished Service Medal](/wiki/Department_of_Transportation_Distinguished_Service_Medal "Department of Transportation Distinguished Service Medal"). The highest unit award available is the [Presidential Unit Citation](/wiki/Presidential_Unit_Citation_(United_States) "Presidential Unit Citation (United States)").[[149]](#cite_note-154)

In wartime, members of the Coast Guard are eligible to receive the Navy version of the Medal of Honor. A Coast Guard Medal of Honor is authorized but has not yet been developed or issued.[[150]](#cite_note-155)

In May 2006, at the Change of Command ceremony when Admiral [Thad Allen](/wiki/Thad_Allen "Thad Allen") took over as Commandant, President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") awarded the entire Coast Guard, including the Coast Guard Auxiliary, the [Coast Guard Presidential Unit Citation](/wiki/Presidential_Unit_Citation_(US) "Presidential Unit Citation (US)") with hurricane device, for its efforts during and after [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina") and [Tropical Storm Rita](/wiki/Hurricane_Rita "Hurricane Rita").[[151]](#cite_note-156)

## Notable Coast Guardsmen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Notable Coast Guardsmen")]

Main article: [List of U.S. Coast Guard people](/wiki/List_of_U.S._Coast_Guard_people "List of U.S. Coast Guard people")

Numerous celebrities have served in the Coast Guard including tennis player [Jack Kramer](/wiki/Jack_Kramer "Jack Kramer"), golfer [Arnold Palmer](/wiki/Arnold_Palmer "Arnold Palmer"), All Star baseball player [Sid Gordon](/wiki/Sid_Gordon "Sid Gordon"), boxer [Jack Dempsey](/wiki/Jack_Dempsey "Jack Dempsey"); surfer and inventor [Tom Blake](/wiki/Tom_Blake_(surfer) "Tom Blake (surfer)"); musicians [Kai Winding](/wiki/Kai_Winding "Kai Winding"), [Rudy Vallee](/wiki/Rudy_Vallee "Rudy Vallee"), [Derroll Adams](/wiki/Derroll_Adams "Derroll Adams"), and [Tom Waits](/wiki/Tom_Waits "Tom Waits"); actors [Buddy Ebsen](/wiki/Buddy_Ebsen "Buddy Ebsen"), [Sid Caesar](/wiki/Sid_Caesar "Sid Caesar"), [Victor Mature](/wiki/Victor_Mature "Victor Mature"), [Richard Cromwell](/wiki/Richard_Cromwell_(actor) "Richard Cromwell (actor)"), [Alan Hale Jr.](/wiki/Alan_Hale_Jr. "Alan Hale Jr."), [William Hopper](/wiki/William_Hopper "William Hopper"), [Beau Bridges](/wiki/Beau_Bridges "Beau Bridges"), [Jeff Bridges](/wiki/Jeff_Bridges "Jeff Bridges"), [Cesar Romero](/wiki/Cesar_Romero "Cesar Romero"); author [Alex Haley](/wiki/Alex_Haley "Alex Haley"); and Senator [Claiborne Pell](/wiki/Claiborne_Pell "Claiborne Pell").

Vice Admiral [Thad Allen](/wiki/Thad_Allen "Thad Allen") in 2005 was named Principal Federal Officer to oversee recovery efforts in the Gulf Region after [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina"). After promotion to Admiral, on the eve of his retirement as Commandant, Allen again received national visibility after being named National Incident Commander overseeing the response efforts of the [Deepwater Horizon oil spill](/wiki/Deepwater_Horizon_oil_spill "Deepwater Horizon oil spill").

Former Coast Guard officers have been appointed to numerous civilian government offices. After retiring as Commandant of the Coast Guard in 2002, Admiral [James Loy](/wiki/James_Loy "James Loy") went on to serve as [United States deputy secretary of homeland security](/wiki/United_States_Deputy_Secretary_of_Homeland_Security "United States Deputy Secretary of Homeland Security"). After their respective Coast Guard careers, [Carlton Skinner](/wiki/Carlton_Skinner "Carlton Skinner") served as the first [civilian governor of Guam](/wiki/Civilian_Governor_of_Guam "Civilian Governor of Guam"); [G. William Miller](/wiki/G._William_Miller "G. William Miller"), 65th [secretary of the treasury](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury"), and retired vice admiral [Harvey E. Johnson Jr.](/wiki/Harvey_E._Johnson_Jr. "Harvey E. Johnson Jr.") served as Deputy Administrator and Chief Operating Officer of the [Federal Emergency Management Agency](/wiki/Federal_Emergency_Management_Agency "Federal Emergency Management Agency") (FEMA) under President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"). Rear Admiral [Stephen W. Rochon](/wiki/Stephen_W._Rochon "Stephen W. Rochon") was appointed by President George W. Bush to serve as the director of the executive residence and [White House chief usher](/wiki/White_House_Chief_Usher "White House Chief Usher"), beginning service on 12 March 2007, and continued to serve in the same capacity under President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama").

Two Coast Guard aviators, Commander [Bruce E. Melnick](/wiki/Bruce_E._Melnick "Bruce E. Melnick") and Captain [Daniel C. Burbank](/wiki/Daniel_C._Burbank "Daniel C. Burbank"), have served as [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") [astronauts](/wiki/Astronauts "Astronauts"). [Coast Guard Reserve](/wiki/Coast_Guard_Reserve "Coast Guard Reserve") commander [Andre Douglas](/wiki/Andre_Douglas "Andre Douglas") was selected in 2021 to join [NASA Astronaut Group 23](/wiki/NASA_Astronaut_Group_23 "NASA Astronaut Group 23").[[152]](#cite_note-Shapiro-157)

Signalman First Class [Douglas Albert Munro](/wiki/Douglas_Albert_Munro "Douglas Albert Munro") was awarded the [Medal of Honor](/wiki/Medal_of_Honor "Medal of Honor") posthumously, and is the only coast guardsman to ever receive this honor.

## Associations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Associations")]

### Coast Guard Aviation Association

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Coast Guard Aviation Association")]

Those who have piloted or flown in Coast Guard aircraft under official flight orders may join the [Coast Guard Aviation Association](/wiki/Coast_Guard_Aviation_Association "Coast Guard Aviation Association") which was formerly known as the "Ancient Order of the Pterodactyl" ("Flying Since the World was Flat"). The Ancient Albatross Award is presented to the active duty USCG member who qualified as an aviator earlier than any other person who is still serving. Separate enlisted and officer awards are given.[[153]](#cite_note-158)[[154]](#cite_note-159)

### Coast Guard CW Operators Association

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Coast Guard CW Operators Association")]

The Coast Guard CW Operators Association (CGCWOA) is a membership organization comprising primarily former members of the United States Coast Guard who held the enlisted rating of Radioman (RM) or Telecommunications Specialist (TC), and who employed International Morse Code (CW) in their routine communications duties on Coast Guard cutters and at shore stations.[[155]](#cite_note-160)

### USCG Chief Petty Officers Association

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: USCG Chief Petty Officers Association")]

Members of this organization unite to assist members and dependents in need, assist with Coast Guard recruiting efforts, support the aims and goals of the Coast Guard Chief Petty Officers Academy, keep informed on Coast Guard matters, and assemble for social amenities; and include Chief, Senior Chief, and Master Chief Petty Officers, active, reserve and retired. Membership is also open to all Chief Warrant Officers and Officers who have served as a Chief Petty Officer.[[156]](#cite_note-161)

### USCG Chief Warrant and Warrant Officers Association (CWOA)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: USCG Chief Warrant and Warrant Officers Association (CWOA)")]

Established in 1929, the Chief Warrant and Warrant Officers Association, United States Coast Guard (CWOA) represents Coast Guard warrant and chief warrant officers (active, reserve and retired) to the Congress, White House and the Department of Homeland Security. Additionally, the association communicates with the Coast Guard leadership on matters of concern to Coast Guard chief warrant officers.[[157]](#cite_note-162)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The U.S. Coast Guard maintains a Motion Picture and Television Office (MOPIC) in [Hollywood, California](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles"), along with its sister services at the [Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") dedicated to enhancing public awareness and understanding of the Coast Guard, its people, and its missions through a cooperative effort with the entertainment industry.[[158]](#cite_note-MOPIC-163)[[159]](#cite_note-Coast_Guard_In_Film-164)

### In film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: In film")]

* *[Don Winslow of the Coast Guard](/wiki/Don_Winslow_of_the_Coast_Guard "Don Winslow of the Coast Guard")* (1943), based on the [Don Winslow of the Navy](/wiki/Don_Winslow_of_the_Navy "Don Winslow of the Navy") comic strip, depicts a Coast Guard intelligence officer hunting down Japanese spies on the west coast of the United States during WWII.
* *[Fighting Coast Guard](/wiki/Fighting_Coast_Guard "Fighting Coast Guard")* (1951), depicts Coast Guard trained to help win WWII.[[159]](#cite_note-Coast_Guard_In_Film-164)
* *[The Boatniks](/wiki/The_Boatniks "The Boatniks")* (1970), a slapstick comedy depicting a clumsy Coast Guard ensign newly assigned as skipper of a cutter in the [Newport Beach](/wiki/Newport_Beach "Newport Beach") area.
* *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian_(2006_film) "The Guardian (2006 film)")* (2006), depicts the [Aviation Survival Technician](/wiki/Aviation_Survival_Technician "Aviation Survival Technician") (AST) program.[[160]](#cite_note-165)
* *[Pain & Gain](/wiki/Pain_%26_Gain "Pain & Gain")* (2013), starring [Dwayne Johnson](/wiki/Dwayne_Johnson "Dwayne Johnson") and [Mark Wahlberg](/wiki/Mark_Wahlberg "Mark Wahlberg"), depicted the Coast Guard [Deployable Specialized Forces](/wiki/Deployable_Specialized_Forces "Deployable Specialized Forces") in action.[[159]](#cite_note-Coast_Guard_In_Film-164)
* *[The Finest Hours](/wiki/The_Finest_Hours_(2016_film) "The Finest Hours (2016 film)")* (2016), A film portraying the rescue of the crew of *[SS Pendleton](/wiki/SS_Pendleton "SS Pendleton")* by [coxswain](/wiki/Coxswain "Coxswain") [Bernard C. Webber](/wiki/Bernard_C._Webber "Bernard C. Webber") and the three other crewmen of *[Coast Guard Motor Lifeboat CG 36500](/wiki/Coast_Guard_Motor_Lifeboat_CG_36500 "Coast Guard Motor Lifeboat CG 36500")*.[[159]](#cite_note-Coast_Guard_In_Film-164)
* *[Deepwater Horizon](/wiki/Deepwater_Horizon_(film) "Deepwater Horizon (film)")* (2016), depicts the events of 20 April 2010 when the mobile drilling platform Deepwater Horizon [suffered a mass casualty explosion](/wiki/Deepwater_Horizon_explosion "Deepwater Horizon explosion") that resulted in the deaths of 11 crew members. The film also depicts the Coast Guard's coordination and response in the immediate aftermath of the explosion.[[161]](#cite_note-166)[[162]](#cite_note-167)

### On television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: On television")]

The Coast Guard has been featured in several [television series](/wiki/Television_series "Television series"), including:

* *[Coast Guard Alaska: Search and Rescue](/wiki/Coast_Guard_Alaska "Coast Guard Alaska")*, a series on [The Weather Channel](/wiki/The_Weather_Channel "The Weather Channel") that features a Coast Guard search-and-rescue unit based in [Kodiak, Alaska](/wiki/Kodiak,_Alaska "Kodiak, Alaska"). Several series have [spun off](/wiki/Spin-off_(media) "Spin-off (media)") the original to focus on units based in [Cape Disappointment](/wiki/Coast_Guard_Station_Cape_Disappointment "Coast Guard Station Cape Disappointment") and [Florida](/wiki/Miami,_Florida "Miami, Florida").[[163]](#cite_note-168)[[164]](#cite_note-169)[[165]](#cite_note-170)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Coast_Guard&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** Although the [U.S. Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") lists its founding as 1775 with the formation of the [Continental Navy](/wiki/Continental_Navy "Continental Navy"), the U.S. Navy was entirely disbanded in 1785. The modern U.S. Navy in its present form was founded in 1794.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** The number of uniformed personnel currently authorized by component. The number actually serving is usually less than the number authorized due to personnel turnover and recruitment efforts that have not filled all available vacancies.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-87)** The term *Coast Guardsman* is the official term used by the U.S. Coast Guard to refer to a member regardless of the person's gender. In an ALCOAST message effective 1 December 2011 the Commandant, Admiral Papp, directed that the language of the [Guardian Ethos](/wiki/U.S._Coast_Guard#The_Guardian_Ethos "U.S. Coast Guard") be superseded by the [Coast Guard Ethos](/wiki/U.S._Coast_Guard#The_Coast_Guard_Ethos "U.S. Coast Guard") in an effort to use terminology that would help with the identity of personnel serving in the Coast Guard. The term *Coast Guardsman* is the correct form of address used in Title 14 USC and is the form that has been used historically.[[84]](#cite_note-ALCOAST_554/11-86)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-94)** 
   * Master Chief Petty Officer of the Coast Guard Reserve Force
   * Command Master Chief Petty Officers for the Deputy Commandants
   * Area Command Master Chief Petty Officers
5. **[^](#cite_ref-135)** For example, the National Park Service operates various vessels with a green racing stripe, as seen at [Channel Islands National Park#Vessels](/wiki/Channel_Islands_National_Park#Vessels "Channel Islands National Park") ([image](https://fineartamerica.com/featured/national-park-service-research-diving-boat-sea-ranger-ii-jason-o-watson.html)) or at [Sleeping Bear Dunes](/wiki/Sleeping_Bear_Dunes "Sleeping Bear Dunes") ([image](https://www.munsonboats.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/08/Munson-Model-26-42-8.jpg)). Other agencies include the [Virginia Beach Police Department Marine Patrol](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Virginia_Beach_Police_Department_Marine_Patrol_Unit_Hull_Number_146.JPG "commons:File:Virginia Beach Police Department Marine Patrol Unit Hull Number 146.JPG"), the [China Coast Guard](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Chinese_Coast_Guard_ship_during_DiREx-15.jpg "commons:File:Chinese Coast Guard ship during DiREx-15.jpg"), the [Turkish Coast Guard](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Turkish_Coast_Guard_Kaan_33_class_patrol_boat_312.jpg "commons:File:Turkish Coast Guard Kaan 33 class patrol boat 312.jpg"), [Baltimore Police Marine Unit](https://www.flickr.com/photos/89903901@N00/47558755441), and the [US Park Police](https://www.nps.gov/subjects/uspp/images/marine-unit.jpg).

"On November 25, 2002, President George Bush signed the Homeland Security Act, which called for the largest reorganization in the U.S. government since the formation of the Department of Defense. The reorganization plan will bring together twenty-two agencies or parts of agencies from other departments such as Justice, Commerce, Health and Human Services, etc., under the control of the Department of Homeland Security.... One of those agencies affected was the United States Coast Guard, which will be transferred from the Department of Transportation."
